reasoning: eter appears paired with ekuer in the line translating as “they ate and drank”; thus eter = ate.
Forms h̬attešnaz/h̬attešnaš occur where the translation mentions the hole; h̬atteššar is the base ‘hole’.
úit occurs in “Hupasiyas … came” and elsewhere; therefore it means came.
illuyankaš is the subject in “the snake came up,” so it corresponds to snake.
našta begins several sentences that translate as “And …,” so it means and.
šarā appears with verbs translated “called up, came up,” so it means up.
ekuer is parallel to eter in “ate and drank”; ekuer = drank.
-aš marks subjects, as in illuyankaš ‘the snake’ when it is the subject.
-ša functions as clitic ‘and,’ e.g., h̬upašiyašša ‘and Hupasiyas,’ dingirmešša ‘and the gods.’
-er/ēr marks 3rd plural past, as in ekuer ‘they drank’ and ninkēr.
-an marks direct objects, e.g., illuyankan ‘the snake’ when it is the object of ‘call/kill.’
-anna is the infinitive marker, as in adanna, akuwanna ‘to eat, to drink.’
-it marks 3rd singular past, as in úit ‘(he) came.’
